data.title: ASML: Timing Matters - Here's Why I'm Waiting
data.description:
ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) crashed 16% on Tuesday and another 5% on Wednesday, marking one of its steepest declines in 20 years, after the company accidentally announced its Q3 2024 earnings a day earlier than previously anticipated. Even worse, the earnings report showed a significant miss on net order bookings and a revision of its previous 2025 guidance, highlighting continued weakness in the equipment-making industry with a recovery now pushed likely beyond the H1 2025.

The report sent shockwaves through the industry, sending other majors such as Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) down 4.7% and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) down 2.6% for the day.

Previous Coverage

Just three weeks ago, I published an article on ASML's stock, labeled "Market Pullback Offers Opportunity, Valued At 25x FY25 Earnings," sharing my bullish view on the lithography machine maker amidst the AI chip production frenzy. I previously expected the US/Dutch government restrictions not to have a material impact on 2025 ASML's earnings as the demand would be compensated by newly built fabs in Europe and the US, but continuous delays are delaying the revenue recognition, while the traditional end markets are recovering slower-than-expected due to cautious consumers, reducing the need for more production capacity buildup in the near-term.

Despite the near-term challenges, particularly in the logic of the slower recovery of end markets such as mobile and PC, the AI story is expected to continue with potential upside in 2025, contrary to some analysts calling this "the end of the AI investment narrative."

The weak guidance is troubling and highlights the larger underlying issues of sufficient capacity in the chip-making business. Yet, ASML's fundamentals remain strong, and investors willing to add shares on the weakness might be well-rewarded once the dust settles. However, reversal may take time, well into mid-2025, to play out, and buying the shares at today's price of €630/share might be "dead money" for a few months. Hence, I am not rushing to buy more shares right away.

The Earnings & Outlook

ASML's early Q3 earnings release, due to a "technical error," left many investors, myself included, with a sour taste. The early earnings release seems like a "rookie mistake" and likely further contributed to the decline in the stock price, given that both order intake and 2025 were major disappointments.

However, the actual Q3 earnings were solid, driven by better-than-expected DUV system sales and higher installed base management fees. Chinese customers frontloaded their orders ahead of anticipated new export restrictions expected to set in from 2025. Overall, ASML reported €7.5B in Net Sales for Q3, surpassing previous analysts' expectations. The net system sales remained well-balanced, with 64% in memory and 36% in logic.

The gross margin remained strong at 50.8%, within ASML's management guidance, thanks to R&D costs being in check and coming below expectations at €1.06B while SG&A of €297M aligned with the guidance. The EPS came at €5.28/share.

ASML closed Q3 with a free cash flow of €534M, a modest improvement from the previous quarter. However, free cash flow remains under pressure due to lower order intake, resulting in fewer down payments and higher inventory levels due to higher inventory build-up of both high-NA and low-NA systems due to longer lead times in the production cycle and preparation for future ramp-up.

Nonetheless, investors' primary concern is ASML's Q3 net booking, which came at just €2.6B, falling short of the estimated €5.6B. This shortfall is a direct result of the slower-than-anticipated recovery of the broader semis market (beyond AI). Yet, it's vital to understand that ASML's backlog is still healthy at €36B, equating to roughly 5.5 quarters of sales if we assume an average of €6.5B worth of backlog is fulfilled each quarter.

Management has shared their viewpoint, acknowledging the demand for AI-chip-making equipment remains strong; the non-AI segments are expected to witness a more gradual recovery than previously anticipated. That means 2024 will be a "transitory year," as previously flagged, and H1 2025 as well, with an expected recovery in H2 2025 and into 2026.

The unfavorable headwind is also reflected in ASML's reduced sales forecast for 2025, now projected to land between €30-35B, compared to the previous projection of as high as €40B, announced back in 2022 during Investor's Day. For Q4, ASML's management is still expected to deliver around €9B in sales (another strong quarter helped by strong demand from China), with 2024 full-year sales now estimated to land around €28B (2% YoY growth compared to 2023, confirming the transitory year narrative). The cautious 2025 view will likely result in 16% sales growth at a midpoint of €32.5B—not a tragedy if you ask me, but enough to spook investors with the guidance coming at the lower end than consensus.

ASML is planning another Investor's Day on November 14, 2024, where management is expected to share their long-term guidance through 2030. This will likely be another key event that sheds light on the momentum of the semis recovery. If you are undecided whether you want to add shares of ASML at today's price, I recommend waiting until this event to gain more clarity, as the stock may remain relatively volatile.

ASML's reduced exposure to the Chinese market will undoubtedly pose some level of headwind in 2025, mainly as Europe and the US are slow in building up new foundries as part of the project to onshore critical infrastructure production, pushing revenue recognition further into H2 2025 and 2026.

As a result of this negative development, analysts have reduced their 2025 EPS growth from the previous 59% growth down to 25% while raising expectations for 2026 as demand remains strong—however, the timing weighs on ASML's near-term growth.

Valuation & Forward Growth

I discussed valuation in great length in my last coverage, so I will provide here just a short update on where the stock is now trading, following the fall from €797 to €629/share following the earnings release.

ASML's shares have traded at an average valuation of around 31.7x in the last 15 years, with an annualized EPS growth of 14% over the same duration.

Following the crash, ASML's shares are now priced at 32.62x its Blended P/E, the earnings of the last two quarters and the next two quarters.

As per FactSet, analysts are now expecting the following EPS growth (for ADR shares):

  • FY2024: EPS of $20.75E, -4% YoY growth
  • FY2025: EPS of $26.00E, 25% YoY growth
  • FY2026: EPS of $32.71E, 26% YoY growth

That's a contraction from the previously expected $32.69 in 2025 and $37.37 in 2026.

I retain my "Strong Buy" rating on ASML's shares, as the expected ROR of around 22% annually over the next 2–3 years is very attractive. However, as I said previously, the stock needs some time to consolidate, and it might be "dead money" for a few months, with some volatility expected around the next Investor's Day.

Personally, I am in no rush to add more shares of ASML as the growth has been pushed beyond 2025, but if the stock drops below €600/share, I will add more shares to dilute the cost basis.

Investor's Takeaway

ASML delivered strong Q3 earnings, but disappointing net booking and 2025 guidance at the lower end of management's previous expectations have led to major disappointment among investors and a sell-off. The early release of the earnings may have exacerbated the pullback even further.

Despite the lower recovery in the non-AI chipmaking segment, demand for AI-related chips remains strong and will likely serve as a tailwind over the following years. Due to normalizing demand and additional export curbs, ASML's dependency on China is expected to drop significantly from 49% this year to 20% next year.

From a fundamental standpoint, ASML's business remains strong but faces adverse macroeconomic headwinds, with the broader semis recovery now pushed into H2 2025 and 2026. In my view, investors do not need to rush to buy ASML's stock, but they should better let the dust settle, potentially waiting for Investor's Day in November, as the stock may witness more volatility.

data.title: After A Month Of Buying, Institutional Investors Back To Selling Stocks
data.description:
The surprising shift by institutional investors to become net buyers of stocks appears to have lasted just one month, as they returned to their positions as the biggest sellers in the equity market in September.

Institutions sold a net $29.93 billion in equities throughout September, their highest level of selling since May and a reversal from August when the group bought nearly $6.82 billion in stocks, according to the latest data from S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Institutions sold a net $241.43 billion from the end of September 2023 to the end of September 2024, even as the S&P 500 rallied more than 34% over that time, the data shows.

"Institutions are adopting a more defensive position as we enter the final quarter of 2024," said Thomas McNamara, a director for Issuer Solutions with S&P Global Market Intelligence. "Several factors are driving this approach, including the upcoming election, ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, Federal Reserve policy, and uncertainty about the presidential outcome."

In spite of these lingering concerns, however, institutions boosted their positions in consumer discretionary sector stocks by 0.4% in September, the top buy for the group for the month. The move into consumer discretionary suggests that institutional investors may have some optimism about the path of the economy as the Fed begins to ease monetary policy through lower interest rates, McNamara said.

Retail investors, meanwhile, reduced their exposure to consumer discretionary stocks by 1.3% in September, the sector that saw the most selling for the group throughout the month.

"Retail investors sought to capitalize on short-term gains, with the sector emerging as the top performer following the Fed's rate cut," McNamara said. "In contrast, institutions, with a longer-term perspective, viewed September as the potential onset of sustained stock price appreciation in the sector, driven by the expectation of continued interest rate reductions."

Overall, capital flows in September seemed to reflect more long running trends. Retail investors sold off $9.38 billion in September, $136.37 billion over the past year; index and exchange-traded funds bought $12.71 billion, $195.93 billion over the course of the year; and hedge funds bought $10.74 billion after buying $22.44 billion in August, continuing to switch from buying and selling each month. Hedge funds have bought a net $8.95 billion since the end of September 2023.

Hedge funds have been employing two divergent strategies recently.

The first strategy, McNamara said, is seizing on trading volatility, buying during stock market dips and selling at potential peaks. This strategy pays little regard to the longer-term direction of the market. The second strategy has hedge funds allocate capital towards more passive investment in order to capture broader market gains as momentum increases in the overall economy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Greenlight Capital Q3 2024 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partner:

The Greenlight Capital funds (the “Partnerships”) returned 1.1%1 in the third quarter of 2024, net of fees and expenses, compared to 5.9% for the S&P 500 index. (SP500,SPX)

There was a lot going on in the world this quarter. Nevertheless, we had a very quiet period, partly because we remained conservatively positioned with very low exposure to equity beta. As we discussed at our Partner Dinner in January, the economy remains relatively strong, with nominal annualized growth now running at 5-6%. On the other hand, however, the market isn’t just making all-time highs. It is, by many measures, the most expensive stock market that we have seen since the founding of Greenlight.

Warren Buffett is, of course, the most successful investor of his generation, and maybe of all time. While Mr. Buffett routinely points out that it is impossible to time the market, we can’t help but observe that he has been one of the best market timers we have ever seen. When the market got too frothy in the late 1960s, he closed his fund. Towards the market bottom in the early 1970s, he re-emerged as a stock picker and then prior to the 1987 crash, he sold everything except a couple of illiquid holdings. Later, he sidestepped the various crises in corporate credit and was well-positioned to capitalize on the 2008 global financial crisis. One could argue that sitting out bear markets has been the underappreciated reason for his outstanding long-term returns. It is therefore noteworthy to observe that Mr. Buffett is again selling large swaths of his stock portfolio and building enormous cash reserves.

Our sense is that Mr. Buffett’s portfolio adjustments are not a prediction that the market will fall next week, next month, or even next quarter. Rather, these stock sales more likely express a long-term view that right now is not a great time to have a lot of equity exposure, and that the opportunity set is expected to be better at some point in the not-so-distant future. We will avoid calling this market a bubble, and simply observe that the dividend yield is low and the P/E ratio is elevated despite corporate earnings being cyclically high, if not top-of-cycle.

This time, it is not just a handful of high-profile technology companies with nosebleed valuations. In fact, it is easy to find mature, industrial businesses with cyclical exposure and growth prospects that aren’t materially better than the current nominal GDP growth rate that currently trade for 30-50x earnings. Obviously, the market appreciation thus far in 2024 has exceeded the growth rate of revenues and earnings. We think Paul Tudor Jones is right when he says that managing the last third of a great bull or bear market move is often the toughest.

However, while we are conservatively positioned with respect to net exposure, we aren’t outright bearish. We are likely to continue to underperform a rising market, as we have all year, but we don’t wish to position ourselves to lose money should the market continue to rise.

Notably, almost all of our return this year has been alpha. While being up 9% year-to-date when the S&P 500 index is up more than 20% over the same period doesn’t feel great, on a beta- adjusted basis we believe we are doing fine. If you happen to be invested in our gold share class, it’s better than fine.

During the quarter, both our longs and shorts rose marginally less than the market. The macro portfolio had another strong quarter, driven by the sharp rise in the price of gold. We also lost money on equity index hedges.

In addition to gold, Green Brick Partners (GRBK) was the other significant winner during the quarter. GRBK reported quarterly earnings of $2.32 per share, which beat analyst estimates of $1.77 per share. The company continues to lead the industry in gross margins and has distinguished itself from its peers, who are increasingly focused on appearing to be “land light.” GRBK believes that being land light is very expensive at the gross margin level. Rather than pay high rates to third-party off-balance sheet land bankers, GRBK owns most of its land and lots while maintaining a very low-leveraged investment-grade balance sheet. As a result, it earns all of the development profits in addition to the homebuilding profits. Despite being “land heavy,” the result is a return on equity that is near the top of the industry. Even more exciting, GRBK has had a very successful period of land acquisition, and as a result the prospects for it to continue delivering industry-leading margins remain promising. This quarter, GRBK shares advanced from $57.24 to $83.52.

On the losing side, ODP Corporation (ODP) reported a second consecutive disappointing quarter. Sales and profits came in well below expectations and the shares reacted by falling from $39.27 to $29.75.

The short book had a material loser in a housing-related short. We believe this company is structurally unprofitable and faces looming debt maturities that could be challenging to refinance at a reasonable interest rate. Nonetheless, it was a strong period for all things housing- related as the sector celebrated the move lower in yields.

While we don’t have any new positions to reveal this quarter, we have identified three new longs that we will likely discuss in the near future. However, as we are still in the accumulation phase, it is premature to describe them now.

We exited a few positions during the quarter, including:

NeuBase Therapeutics (NBSE): We described this nascent company in our Q4 2020 letter. Ultimately, NBSE ran out of money and failed. We do like to have a few small lottery tickets in the portfolio, but this one didn’t hit. Our NBSE investment was less than 1% at cost, which we lost most of over our five-year holding period.

NatWest Group (United Kingdom: NWG): We sold after achieving a 20% IRR over almost two years. The thesis played out and we no longer judged the shares to be materially undervalued.

Ryanair (Ireland: RYAAY): We exited with a modest profit after three and a half years. For some time, the post-COVID environment was favorable, allowing the company to raise prices and improve its earnings. Alas, that turned. Recently, pricing has reversed and earnings have been disappointing. As a result, we wound up surrendering what had been some decent unrealized gains.

At the end of the quarter, we said goodbye to David Tepperman who decided to pursue new adventures. David was with us for over 15 years, and we want to thank him for the long period of contribution.

On September 23, Toby and Ruth Haselberger welcomed the newest addition to the Greenlight family with the birth of Gia Isabella. Big sister Coco was thrilled to meet her baby sister “Bella.” Congratulations to the Haselbergers!

Please save the date for our 29 th Annual Partner Dinner, which will be held on January 21, 2025 in New York.

At quarter-end, the largest disclosed long positions in the Partnerships were Brighthouse Financial (BHF), CONSOL Energy (CEIX), Green Brick Partners (GRBK), HP and Solvay (OTCPK:SVYSF). The Partnerships had an average exposure of 97% long and 64% short.

“You are allowed to be both a masterpiece and a work in progress, simultaneously.” – Sophia Bush

Best Regards,

Greenlight Capital

data.title: Interpublic Group: May Not Even Have The Growth Edge Anymore
data.description:
Interpublic Group of Companies (NYSE:IPG), or IPG for short, put in a decent performance as of the last earnings some months ago with modest topline performance and a bit of operating profit growth. This is in line with our previous coverage where we also noted solid cost performance despite pretty limited topline growth action, which is consistent with the current macro environment which pressures ad spend, but also supports the company's ambitious guidance for meaningful EBITA margin improvement for the FY. They confirmed the growth downgrade in the Q2 due to a reduction in some of the healthcare markets with an important client scaling back spend in March, and this had compromised a better growth outlook on the back of a recovery in tech which has been a weaker market so far which could have been the clincher for overcoming toughening comps in H2. The company's focus on the North American market had at least kept growth in the cards, particularly as a lagging tech and telco market looks to recover towards the end of the year. Europe has also been an idiosyncratically strong performer overall for IPG as of Q2.

However, while they are a solid pick operationally, and at least avoid excessive and potentially troublesome Chinese exposure, they lost some more business in September to their competitors. This means growth guidance should be further compromised and that should become evident in the Q4, and might affect guidance comments in the coming Q3 earnings. Even more importantly, they are pricier than peer WPP (WPP) in terms of forward PE, and we think that in a very mature and competitive industry like advertising, the value of growth opportunities are more marginal in the valuation picture and the help of a discounted multiple is more important in gunning for a return. With the combination of the recent business losses that may cloud announcements and the more premium multiple, we prefer other picks within the sector over IPG.

Q2 earnings and looking to upcoming Q3

The biggest IPG market is the US at around 66% of net revenues in the Q2. The US consumer remained strong even after the close of books, but there was still some general pressure in terms of ad spending by customers with reticence around the US market by US corporates. Growth was 1.3% organically YoY for the US which was in line with peers and reflects a pretty solid macro backdrop in the US with the consumer there still strong, but it was in the end the international markets that saw more powerful growth at 2.6% organically.

This international performance was not really consistent with peers, where Europe was generally a bit shaky, and was even trending down in the UK and the Western parts of Europe for WPP.

IPG saw acceleration and strong performance, owing apparently to a couple of large accounts that have an idiosyncratic effect on the business. The UK grew 3.4% organically where PR saw solid growth, and continental Europe grew a very solid 6.3% marking considerable share wins over more focused European players like indeed WPP where these markets in the same quarter either saw more limited growth or outright shrank.

The delta between the European and UK performance and overall ex-US performance comes from APAC which was down a clear 5% or so, consistent with the drag from macro-pressured geographies like China which are likely to remain a problem. In general, it should be noted that all these businesses, focused on the spending wallet for advertising at corporates, is pretty sensitive to macro and not recession resistant, as advertising is in itself both clearly cyclical and also discretionary. It's often basically at the click of a button that corporates can dial back advertising spend. That's why we are not crazy about APAC exposure right now.

Guided growth was still expected to be relatively flat in terms of the topline despite solid growth so far in the year as of the Q2 call. This is consistent with the expectations of IPG peers as well, even those with less favourable markets in the mix, namely WPP which has a much larger Chinese and APAC business (approx. 25% of sales compared to 7% for IPG) that is expected to be a challenging market into the end of the year and beyond due to the property deleveraging situation. However, the similar growth guidance is for different reasons. There was an idiosyncratic hit that tapered growth earlier on in the year for IPG.

On our last call with you, we noted that due to a decision by a major and ongoing health care client in late March related to their global consumer advertising work, the high end of the 1% to 2% growth range, that we had targeted as we entered the year would not be achievable... [1% guidance in effect].

Philippe Krakowsky, CEO of IPG Q2 2024 Call

There was also a call-out in the last quarter that the macro environment is looking a bit tougher going forward. IPG, as opposed to WPP, also has tougher comps going into the H2, with H2 2023 having been pretty solid at IPG with considerable sequential topline development ahead of WPP's in percentage growth. From March to December 2023, WPP had less than 10% sequential improvement, while IPG had around 15%.

We expect that tech in particular, which has been a weak area across advertising so far this year, should see a bit of a rebound towards the end of the year and support growth where some was idiosyncratically lost in an important health account at IPG.

Going the other way, we saw decreases in the financial services, tech and telecom and auto and transportation sectors.

Ellen Johnson, CFO of IPG Q2 2024 Call

And then on the tech sector, we talked about the stabilization that we have seen in Q2. And we would expect to see a movement into growth in the second half.

Joanne Wilson, CFO of WPP Q2 2024 Call

The same rebound effect was mentioned by peers looking forward and should be lifting them as well as we come around to the Q3.

WPP was looking at something like 0-1% in guided growth for the FY as of their Q2 call. IPG was guiding only slightly ahead of that, just north of 1%, on the basis of a healthcare account that has shrunk on a go forward basis but also notably tougher comps. IPG's end markets geographically are healthier than WPP's, which is more levered to China which is having protracted macroeconomic problems. However, with the announcement of lost business from Amazon (AMZN) in September, the growth guidance should deteriorate further, and is likely now slightly in negative territory which could even put it behind peers like WPP despite WPP's more challenged APAC exposures.

In terms of guidance, WPP is looking at around 5-6% operating profit improvement coming in from just the cost savings plan in 2024. IPG is guiding a 16.6% EBITA margin for the FY, with the Q2 running at 14.6% meaning an expectation of significant improvement. In addition to concerns around the topline guidance, profit guidance could also be impacted, since 180 bps in margin improvement YoY in the Q2 came from operating leverage on payroll. Those positive effects could go into partial reverse if growth indeed ends up negative due to the recently lost business in September.

Bottom Line

FGS Global saw a partial sale to KKR (KKR) which has helped us at least with a precedent for valuing PR businesses at least, and brought some absolute appeal due to low multiples on the residual businesses.

We didn't see specific disclosure as far as PR revenues at IPG, which gets more clearly broken out at WPP, but IPG's specialised communications segment at around 15% of revenues for IPG does contain the PR business. The PR businesses at WPP are valued at around 20x EV/EBITDA multiples based on the FGS precedent. Assuming the same mix of PR revenues at IPG as at WPP, its own non-PR businesses could be valued at around 6.4x. WPP's non-PR businesses are at around 5.2x. Note that effective tax rates between the two companies are similar enough to not be able to explain the extent of the difference in those multiples. Also, annualising PEs at IPG and assuming a normal tax rate you get something like a 15-16x forward PE compared to WPP at around 12-13x. The difference is similar in multiple when using Seeking Alpha's consensus forecast-based forward PEs, around 11-12x for IPG versus 8-9x for WPP (from different sources and forecast pools it's around 12.2x IPG to 11.4x for WPP on a forward PE basis).

IPG benefits in that WPP has more undesirable APAC exposure, but with ultimately pretty similar growth guidance as of the Q2, and with WPP and Omnicom (OMC) outcompeting IPG for some important business from Amazon, WPP's outlook is improving at this point with the IPG outlook deteriorating. With the announcement of this lost business being recent, we are not terribly excited about IPG's upcoming earnings next week as the modest growth guidance will probably be further tempered and operating leverage benefits that drove margin performance in Q2 potentially reversing.

We had strong leverage on base payroll which helped drive 180 basis points of operating leverage on salaries and related expenses compared to a year ago...

Philippe Krakowsky, CEO of IPG

We have concerns around China and APAC at WPP, where macro pressures are more protracted, but we'd rather seek refuge within the sector in a stock with more margin of safety thanks to a discounted multiple. So we eschew IPG.

data.title: Puig Brands: A Long-Term Buy At These Levels
data.description:
Introduction

Puig Brands (OTCPK:PUIGF) (OTCPK:PUGBY) is a Spanish company focusing on the higher end market of the fragrance, make-up and skincare aspects of consumer products. The company owns the Rabanne, Carolina Herrera and Jean-Paul Gaultier brands which have worldwide brand recognition. For a good overview of Puig’s activities, I’d recommend you to read my previous article on the company.

Puig Brands is trading on the Madrid Stock Exchange with PUIG as its ticker symbol. While the average daily volume is approximately half a million shares per day, I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

Unfortunately, the Puig website contains download-only links, but you can find all relevant information I will be referring to here.

The free cash flow remained strong in the first semester

In the first semester of the current financial year, Puig Brands reported total revenue of 2.17B EUR, an increase of around 10% compared to the first half of last year. Meanwhile, the COGS increased by just over 31M EUR, representing an increase of around 6%, which indeed meant the gross profit increased at a faster pace than the revenue. Whereas the company recorded a 1.49B EUR revenue in the first half of last year, this has now jumped to 1.65B EUR in the first semester of the current financial year. That’s an increase of almost 11%.

Of course, some of the other operating expenses increased as well. The income statement above clearly shows how the company stepped up its marketing efforts as the advertising and promotion expenses increased by roughly 17% to 685M EUR. Although distribution expenses decreased, the other SG&A expenses increased, and this resulted in an operating profit of just under 313M EUR. This represents a 3% increase compared to the first half of last year, which is fine, but not very exciting.

The income statement above also shows a 135M EUR "other operational expense" which definitely weighed on the bottom line result. The vast majority of those expenses were related to the IPO (shown below). The "other" operating expenses are predominantly related to sponsorships of events.

Fortunately, the majority of those expenses also allowed to reduce the taxable income, and the sharply lower taxes owed based on the H1 result saved the day somewhat. The net profit was 156.8M EUR, and after deducting the 3M EUR in net income attributable to non-controlling interests, the net profit attributable to the Puig shareholders was 153.8M EUR. There are approximately 568M shares outstanding, resulting in an underlying EPS of 0.27 EUR per share.

This doesn’t sound like the current share price of close to 19 EUR is a good deal, but keep in mind the IPO-related expenses reduced the pre-tax income by approximately 40%. This also means the underlying EPS would likely have exceeded 40 cents per share.

Looking at the cash flow statement, the reported operating cash flow was a negative 191.2M EUR, but this included a 386.6M EUR investment in the working capital position. We should also deduct the 28M EUR in lease payments from the equation, resulting in an adjusted operating cash flow of 167.5M EUR.

The total capex was just 65.6M EUR, resulting in a net free cash flow of 101M EUR. Again, this does not represent the true underlying operating and free cash flow, given the sizeable cash outflow related to non-recurring items. I expect the second half of the year to have a more pronounced reported free cash flow result.

The more visible metrics like the adjusted EBITDA increased substantially: The H1 adjusted EBITDA jumped by 7.4% to 410M EUR while the net debt remained stable at 1.5B EUR, representing a multiple of just 1.7 times the adjusted EBITDA. Keep in mind, the company exercised a 215M EUR option to acquire an additional stake in one of its brands subsequent to the end of the first semester, and this will be visible in the full-year results. That being said, I also anticipate a portion of the working capital investment to be reversed, which means the net debt should actually decrease by the end of this year.

Investment thesis

The company’s H1 results were deformed by the in excess of 100M EUR in non-recurring expenses related to getting the company ready for its IPO. This shouldn’t be re-occurring in the second half of the year, so I'm expecting a substantial increase in the reported net income. I’m also looking forward to seeing a guidance for 2025 and comparing it with the consensus estimates of 5.1B EUR in revenue and 1.03B EUR in EBITDA. The forward EV/EBITDA of 11 for FY 2025 makes Puig Brands an appealing option in the luxury segment.

I currently have no position in Puig Brands, but I have written put options and will continue to do so, as I wouldn’t mind picking up a long position around the current share price levels. A P18 expiring in December, for instance, currently offers an option premium of approximately 0.50 EUR, which would allow one to buy the stock at an average of 17.50 EUR should the option expire in the money.

data.title: Canada Debates Whether To Supersize Rate Cuts
data.description:
By James Knightley and Francesco Pesole

Close call between 25bp and 50bp

The Bank of Canada has lowered the target for the overnight rate at its past three policy meetings and is expected to follow up with a fourth consecutive cut next week. The question is, will it be another 25bp move or will the bank follow the Federal Reserve’s lead and cut by 50bp?

Consumer price inflation slowed to just 1.6% year-on-year in September, undershooting the 2% target for the first time since February 2021. The bulk of the residual strength is due to housing costs, which, if excluded, would prompt inflation to fall below 1%. Nonetheless, we can’t pick and choose which things to exclude and include, and BoC officials remain wary about easing too far, too fast and running the risk of a return of price pressures.

At the September policy meeting, the committee argued that "excess supply in the economy continues to put downward pressure on inflation, while price increases in shelter and some other services are holding inflation up. The Governing Council is carefully assessing these opposing forces on inflation".

Inflation is cooling, the labour market is loosening, but the growth outlook is improving

In terms of the job market situation, unemployment has climbed from a low of 4.8% in July 2022 to 6.5% today, but this is not because of job losses. Total employment is actually at all-time highs and stands more than a million higher than pre-pandemic levels. Instead, it is down to excess supply resulting from rapid population increase generated by a large wave of immigration in recent years. Nonetheless, this slack in the labour market should keep a lid on wage pressures and help contain inflation pressures over the medium term.

General activity levels are subdued but the economy is growing, with recent monthly GDP and retail sales numbers beating expectations and the third-quarter Business Outlook survey pointing to a more optimistic outlook for the Canadian economy. As such, we continue to see a strong case for ongoing gradual rate cuts to bring monetary policy to a more neutral setting. Whether the committee sees enough to justify an acceleration in the process with a 50bp move is a close call.

Widening US-Canada rate differentials could argue for caution

The consensus is on a knife edge as to whether it will be a 25bp or a 50bp cut. Financial markets are more confident, pricing 45bp of a 50bp move, with a cumulative 83bp of cuts priced over the November and December meetings versus a mere 60bp just over a week ago.

A 50bp cut from the BoC would widen out the spread with the Fed funds target rate to 125bp once again, and this may put additional downside pressures on the Canadian dollar. This would threaten to push up import costs and may also add to nervousness from some officials about pursuing such a move. Consequently, we very narrowly favour the BoC choosing to go with 25bp, but would not be shocked by a 50bp cut.

CAD: Lots of dovishness in the price

The gap between the USD and CAD two-year swap rate has widened substantially of late, from 50bp to 80bp since the start of October. This is mirroring both aggressive dovish rate expectations in Canada and a hawkish repricing in the US. Such a widening gap has been the main driver for the USD/CAD rally to the 1.38 mark and has prevented the loonie from benefitting from its lower risk profile in the crosses, ahead of some pre-US election positioning.

The balance of risks is clearly skewed to the upside for CAD next week. Markets are fully pricing in a 50bp move that is far from guaranteed, and a 25bp cut can trigger a sizeable correction higher in the loonie. Even in the event of a half-point cut, markets are pricing in more than 25bp in December, meaning the room for a further dovish repricing in the CAD OIS curve is low. Our view remains that CAD has room to appreciate, if not against USD that might get support into the US election, at least versus other commodity currencies (e.g. AUD, NZD) that are more exposed to Trump hedges.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Why Nokia Fell After Posting Q3 2024 Earnings (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In the past four months, Nokia Oyj (NYSE:NOK) gained around 13%, beating the S&P 500’s (SP500) return of 3.6%. NOK stock broke out below $4.00 after reporting second-quarter results.

For the third quarter, the network infrastructure firm posted revenue falling from the previous year. In addition, Nokia did not raise its outlook. Following that, NOK stock traded down by around 5% in pre-market trade.

Is the reaction justified? The stock ran up before the report and risks giving back some of that gain.

Nokia Q3 2024 Results

In the third quarter, Nokia reported earnings of EUR 0.06 (US $0.065) per share. Revenue fell by 8.1% year-on-year to EUR 4.33 billion (US $4.7 billion). Chief Executive Officer Pekka Lundmark said in the earnings press release that he is optimistic that the company is turning the corner in many parts of its business.

Net sales in Network Infrastructure grew in the quarter. Order backlog grew in the period. Fixed Networks grew by 9%, while IP Networks revenue grew by 6%. Overall, the Network Infrastructure net sales fell by 1.0% Y/Y. Gross margin increased to 42.1%, compared to 40.5% last year. This indicated that the company is not chasing low-profit or money-losing contracts.

Still, looking ahead, the firm issued a negative net sales growth forecast for the network segment. It expects negative growth of up to -6.0%. Despite falling revenue, this unit accounts for the highest operating margin in the double-digit percentage.

The CEO highlighted the excellent momentum in 5G Core, suggesting potential growth from network automation, cloudification, and enabling network APIs. It will capture opportunities by expanding its presence in the data center market. To achieve that goal, it will invest to broaden its product portfolio in IP Networks.

The business from Nokia Technologies continued to stabilize. The firm concluded its smartphone renewal cycle. It will seek business expansion into new growth areas.

Opportunities

Nokia proposed an acquisition of Infinera (INFN) for $2.3 billion on June 27, 2024. This will likely close after the HSR waiting period for the Justice Department’s review has expired. The up to $93 million funding from the CHIPS Act is a positive tailwind for Infinera. The combination of investment tax credits and direct funding could total over $200 million in federal incentives.

Traders may view the impact of CHIPS Act funding on a stock. Wolfspeed (WOLF) received $1.5 billion in total funding. It will get up to $750 million from the CHIPS Act and $750 million from a consortium of investment funds led by Apollo (APO).

I recently suggested in this tax-loss selling article that Wolfspeed is a stock to hold.

Shareholder Returns

On October 17, the Board of Directors resolved to pay out a dividend of EUR 0.03 a share to shareholders of record on October 22. To return up to EUR 600 million, Nokia declared a buyback program in January 2024. On July 19, it accelerated the timeframe of the buyback program. It will complete the EUR 600 million buyback by the end of this year, compared to the original two-year timeline.

The company is paying for Infinera with up to 30% in stock. To offset dilution, The Board committed to buy back more stock. This is on top of the current EUR 600 million plan. So far, the company bought back 84.29 million shares at an average price of EUR 3.48 (US $3.77). In comparison, NOK stock closed at $4.46 on Oct. 16. At the time of writing, shares will trade at around $4.20.

Risks

Q3 net sales fell mostly from 75% of the decline driven by India. Nokia reported stronger results in the year-ago period, hurting the comparable results. Despite risks of wider negative revenue growth, gross margins are expanding. Nokia improved its product and regional mix. It also cut product costs. The continued drive for higher efficiency and lowering operating expenses will raise its profitability.

Expect Nokia to manage the continued intense competition without sacrificing profitability. It will win contracts by remaining disciplined on price. The more it reduces its costs, the better it may compete on lower product pricing.

NOK Stock Grade

Income investors should consider Nokia’s weak dividend grades. While the stock yields 3.13%, the stock has weak dividend safety, growth, and consistency. Despite the Infinera acquisition, the dividend safety and growth grades improved from three months ago:

Nokia’s valuation is compelling. It scored an A- on that factor grade:

Looking closely at its growth, negative revenue growth is hurting its growth grade.

Growth is negative on every metric, compared to the positive figures from the sector median. The operating cash flow growth, however, is a bright spot. Nokia is cutting costs and growing profit margins. Operating FCF grew threefold, compared to the average of 17.47%.

According to Stock Rover, Nokia shares have a fair value of $4.59. This is based on the margin of safety, which is a difference between a company's fair value and its price.

In comparison, Wall Street has a $4.92 target price on NOK stock.

Your Takeaway

Nokia’s post-earnings decline is justified. The stock may trade at more than 6% lower as markets proverbially “sell the stock on the news.” In the near term, Nokia may trade back to its historical range of $3.75 — $4.25.

To break out above $4.00, Nokia needs to report revenue growth in all of its sectors. The gross margin improvement may not last after the company cannot find more cost cuts. Competitors will pressure Nokia’s product prices. As a result, net sales from Network Infrastructure need to increase in the next year.

data.title: Fiduciary Management Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Outlook
data.description:
Domestic and international equity markets rose in the September quarter, capping off an exceptional run over the past 12 months. With gains of 36.35% in the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) , 26.76% in the Russell 2000 Index (RTY), and 24.77% in the MSCI EAFE Index ('USD'), the indices far outpaced historical average returns. Inflation has moderated and several central banks have started easing, with the Federal Reserve (Fed), European Central Bank (ECB), and Bank of England (BoE) each cutting interest rates. Investors celebrated the accommodations, pushing already stretched valuations to new heights.

As we take a step back, we are struggling to reconcile the stock market euphoria with much of the fundamental backdrop. That said, there are always going to be relative and absolute values in the market, and it is our job to find these regardless of the environment. We welcome volatility in the days, weeks, and months ahead, as it creates compelling long-term opportunities for the portfolios. While there are certainly pockets of strength in the global economy, such as those entities with exposure to mega cap tech (artificial intelligence/AI), obesity drugs, infrastructure, and defense spending (among the top-performing themes in the U.S. and Europe in 2024), there is also evidence building on the negative side of the ledger.

In the U.S., the unemployment rate has risen to 4.2% (from 3.7% in January), and GDP growth is expected to slow to 1.6% in 2025 (from 2.6% in 2024), according to OECD forecasts. Housing, manufacturing, transportation, technology (outside of AI capital expenditures) and discretionary spending have weakened. The low-income consumer is under significant pressure, as several years of steep inflation have taken a toll. Excess savings have been depleted and credit delinquencies, while still low by historical standards, are on the rise. Overseas, the economic backdrop is also softening. The ECB recently cut their growth forecasts for each of the next three years, with subpar household consumption and a slump in manufacturing weighing on demand. In Japan, growth is expected to shrink in 2024 before recovering modestly. China launched several stimulus programs to try to reinvigorate growth that has slowed.

Meanwhile, geopolitical risks remain high, with an expanding conflict in the Middle East, no end in sight for the Russia/Ukraine war, and aggressive Chinese posturing toward Taiwan. By the end of 2024, it will have been an election year for half of the world’s GDP, creating further consternation. In the interim, government deficits are ballooning, and debt levels are hitting new all-time highs. As shown in the chart above, global debt reached $315 trillion this year, up almost $100 trillion since 2016.

Unfortunately, equity markets do not appear to be accounting for global risks or external shocks. Valuations are seemingly priced for perfection, as earnings growth has not kept up with stock prices, leaving little margin for error. We have highlighted the S&P 500 characteristics in several prior letters, which in essence has become a concentrated growth index driven by a handful of anointed companies. Many S&P 500 multiples are in the 10th decile (most expensive) of historical valuation, with several metrics virtually unrecognizable from their long-term norms (see below):

Has U.S. economic growth justified this optimism? Will it in the future? U.S. GDP growth has been below average since the Great Financial Crisis, and with high debt burdens and difficult demographics, projected growth would not seem to warrant near all-time high valuations.

Not surprisingly, growth has outperformed value across the board in the current fiscal year, with the gap most acute in U.S. Large Cap, where the Russell 1000 Growth Index gained 42.19% versus 27.76% for the Value. As can be seen in the 50-year chart below, growth has been on a tear since 2007, after lagging by a significant margin from 1974-2007. It’s important to remember that markets can move in very long cycles, but trees (and growth stocks) do not grow to the sky. We are confident value investing will have a strong resurgence in the fullness of time, as valuation and risk aversion will eventually return to the forefront.

Despite nosebleed valuations, investors continue to pile into the U.S. stock market, chasing performance. Greed and a fear of missing out will likely lead to steady buying all the way to the eventual tipping point. As detailed in the August 2nd Grant’s Interest Rate Observer, “Inflows into passive growth funds in 2024 [are] on pace to double vs. 2023, which was already the highest year on record.” Per J.P. Morgan, stock allocations in U.S. households recently accounted for 42% of their total financial assets, the most on record dating back to 1952. Risk appetites appear to be showing no signs of abating.

As the market seemingly celebrates new daily record highs, it feeds off a never-ending obsession with the Fed’s every move. It’s widely accepted that rate cuts are good news for the economy and stock market. We have a different perspective. As we wrote in our September 2022 letter: “Did the move from ‘normal’ interest rates prior to the Great Financial Crisis to zero percent rates for years afterwards cause the economy to boom? No! Economic growth was weak over the ensuing 15 years. Abnormally low rates boosted asset prices and set the mergers and acquisitions (M&A) and private equity markets aflame, but it did very little for organic economic growth. We had a boom in financial engineering.” We do not believe you can cut your way to prosperity.

There Are Still Values to be Had

Our long-time readers know that FMI has typically lagged in more speculative-driven bull markets (similar to today’s) and outperformed in more challenging environments, a direct result of our valuation framework, focus on business quality, and balance sheet strength. Many investors today have never seen an extended bear market, and repeatedly have been rewarded for buying the dip. At some point this dynamic will change, as will risk tolerance. In the meantime, we will remain cautious, trying to find the best available values amidst the opportunity set.

While valuations are elevated across market caps and geographies, fortunately it’s less extreme outside of U.S. mega cap technology. Compared with the Magnificent Seven (Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Tesla, and Meta Platforms), the other 493 stocks in the S&P 500 trade at ~31% lower valuations, using a basket of metrics. The FMI Large Cap strategy trades at a ~30% and ~50% discount to the 493 and Magnificent Seven, respectively, for what we believe are well-run, above-average businesses1.

For comparison, profitable small cap companies in the U.S. trade near their widest discount to large caps in over 40 years, aside from the 2000 tech bubble. In recent years, incredible gains and concentration in the technology sector have been a key large cap driver. With the Nasdaq Composite index (COMP:IND, a proxy for technology) gaining 44.70% in 2023 and 21.84% in the first nine months of 2024, small cap companies (and nearly everything else) have failed to keep pace. As illustrated in the chart below, make no mistake that this dynamic can flip in a hurry, something tech investors from 2000 would like to – and seem to currently – forget.

The FMI Small Cap strategy has had a very strong run in recent years but gave up some modest ground in the most recent quarter, as money-losing companies and financials (lower quality and/or highly levered) drove the Russell 2000. We estimate that the FMI Small Cap strategy trades at a ~37% discount to the Russell 20001. We feel great about our relative positioning when compared to the subpar business quality throughout the benchmark.

While still high from a historical perspective, overseas stock valuations are not nearly as egregious as their American counterparts. Many comparable companies in Europe and Asia trade at meaningful discounts to U.S. peers. Last quarter, we questioned whether the Japanese market had gotten a bit ahead of itself. On cue, the Nikkei 225 Index (NKY:IND) came under pressure in the September quarter, and much of the headwind we faced earlier in the year from our significant underweight in Japan has abated. The FMI International strategy continues to trade at a discount to the MSCI EAFE Index.

With heightened risks and demanding valuations, prudence and patience are warranted. Our clients can take comfort that FMI has a long history of downside protection in more difficult markets. We expect that our discipline will pay dividends when those who have been less discerning and chasing returns ultimately face the music, which is playing louder with each passing quarter.

Listed below are a few portfolio holdings where we see attractive absolute and relative values:

Arrow Electronics Inc. (ARW) – Small Cap/All Cap

Arrow is a distributor of electronic components and enterprise computing solutions. As a distributor, the company benefits from global technology growth without being directly exposed to technology inventor’s risk. Arrow has a diversified set of vendors, customers, and end-markets, as the company serves over 100,000 customers in 85 countries, including thousands of value-added resellers (VARs). No single customer accounts for more than 2% of sales, and their biggest supplier accounts for 10% of sales. The company has performed well over the long-term, growing sales while earning a return on invested capital ('ROIC') that exceeds its cost of capital. Today, Arrow trades near its historical P/E multiple, yet earnings are depressed due to lower technology spending. Additionally, the company trades at a little over book value, which is below its historical average. While there is some cyclicality in Arrow’s end markets, we like the counter-cyclical free cash flow profile of the business combined with management’s capital allocation priorities. The company has been advantageously repurchasing its stock, helping earnings per share to increase at attractive rates that are much higher than operating profits. Over the past five years, shares outstanding have decreased significantly, with EPS compounding in the teens. The valuation multiples are attractive given the business quality and growth prospects.

Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) – Large Cap/All Cap/Global/ Focused Global

Berkshire Hathaway is a diversified holding company that owns subsidiaries in insurance and reinsurance, freight rail transportation, utilities, energy, manufacturing, services, retail, and finance. Chairman, controlling shareholder, and famed investor Warren Buffett uses the permanent capital base of the large insurance assets – along with disciplined underwriting standards – to drive value-added investments that compound shareholder returns over time. From its inception in 1965 through 2023, Berkshire’s market value has increased at an annual growth rate of 19.8% vs. 10.2% for the S&P 500. Berkshire’s primary assets are its large insurance businesses, including GEICO, General Re, and National Indemnity, which we estimate at ~35% of the company’s value. The enduring competitive advantages of Berkshire’s insurance assets have allowed it to increase its float at a higher rate than its peers and provide low-cost funding for a strong collection of operating businesses, including BNSF Railroad, McLane Foodservice, Pilot Travel, Marmon Industries, Dairy Queen, and many others. On conservative estimates, we believe the company is trading at over a 20% discount on a sum- of-the-parts basis, and carries a mid-teens earnings multiple when factoring in a healthy net cash position and look-through EPS from its public equity portfolio. In an expensive market, we find the valuation to be significantly more appealing than the S&P 500. We expect a combination of strong operations and value- added acquisitions to increase the intrinsic value of the company at an above-average rate going forward.

Informa PLC (OTCPK:IFPJF) – International

Informa is a leader in corporate events and operates an attractive core business in organizing and operating industry trade shows across the globe. The firm has grown through both organic and inorganic show development, building defensible positions serving attractive industry niches. Informa’s competitive advantages stem from the network effects inherent to its flagship offerings, scale advantages in procurement, and venue access that the firm enjoys over smaller rivals. As the events business continues to normalize post-pandemic, we expect a period of above-normal revenue growth and margin expansion to persist over the coming years. On a normalized basis, the firm’s financial profile is characterized by mid-single-digit revenue growth and low- double-digit EPS growth. We believe there is plenty of room for Informa to continue to take share in this highly fragmented global market, with management’s shifting capital allocation priorities providing a kicker to shareholder returns. A more conservatively managed balance sheet, consistent share buyback activity, and a resumption of its growing dividend have been welcome developments as the firm emerges from the industrywide shock caused by the pandemic. We were able to purchase shares at a mid-teens earnings multiple, which we believe undervalues the through-cycle growth potential available at this high-quality franchise.

Thank you for your continued support of Fiduciary Management, Inc.

Footnotes

1 Estimated valuations are calculated utilizing iShares ETF constituent level data as index level proxies. Calculations are based on the estimated valuations by looking at the average discount to the weighted average valuations as of 9/30/2024 for the trailing 1-year Price-to-Earnings Ratio, Fiscal Year One Price-to-Earnings Ratio, Fiscal Year Two Price-to- Earnings Ratio, Price-to-Sales Ratio, and Price-to-Book Ratio. Valuations are adjusted based on criteria identified by FMI. For more information, please contact FMI.

data.title: Sangoma Technologies: A Turnaround Showing Solid Progress
data.description:
Investment thesis

Having a market leading product platform, Sangoma Technologies (NASDAQ:SANG) differentiates itself from its competitors by being a single-vendor for its customers, catering to all their cloud communication needs at an affordable price. Its offering is mainly aimed at the SMB market, which the company estimates to be a $33 billion opportunity. Following the change in its go-to-market strategy implemented last year, the company is prepared to move further upmarket to tackle the needs of slightly larger customers. The success of this approach is reflected in its share price, which has doubled so far this year. Despite this significant share price appreciation, I find the current valuation of 5.7 times FCF very attractive, especially compared to its peers. While the outlook for growth remains muted, I expect earnings growth will remain strong due to likely margin expansion from FY26 onwards. Consequently, I rate SANG stock a Buy.

Reviewing recent results following the business transformation

Before Charles Salameh became CEO of Sangoma in September 2023, the company faced significant challenges, such as declining revenue, falling margins and a heavy debt burden, which resulted in its share price falling more than 90% from its peak in early 2021. A key element of the turnaround strategy he implemented was a transformation of the company's go-to-market approach.

Sangoma's diverse product offerings in the cloud communication market, including hardware, Unified Communications as a Service (UCaaS), Contact Center as a Service (CCaaS), Communications Platform as a Service (CPaaS) and SIP Trunking were restructured into a unified Sangoma brand. This bundling of products from prior acquisitions prioritized sales of higher-margin services, which achieve gross margins near 70% and churn rates below 1%. This strategy also allowed the company to better leverage demand generation from its strategic sales partners, enabling Sangoma’s sales team to collaborate more effectively with these partners.

By bundling hardware, software, and managed services, Sangoma positioned itself to better serve larger clients, thus moving upmarket and allowing it to build a more stable base of recurring service revenue. Recognizing its low brand recognition among end customers due to reliance on marketing partners, management has also made concerted efforts to enhance brand awareness.

The results from these actions appear to be gradually bearing fruit, as shown in the results above. Revenue declined 4% year over year in the final quarter of FY24, mainly driven by lower hardware sales due to macroeconomic headwinds. Meanwhile, services revenue remained flat and accounted for 81% of total revenue. Adjusted EBITDA rose 2% to $11.1 million at a margin of 18.2%, benefiting from cost reduction initiatives taken by management earlier this year, which resulted in annual cost savings of $9 million. Furthermore, due to prudent inventory management, cash from operating activities in Q4 rose 8% year over year to $11.7 million, supporting the company's deleveraging process.

Looking ahead: What investors can expect

Entering FY25 in a more stable financial position helped by the strong profitability of the business, management expressed optimism with full-year guidance implying 3% organic growth at the midpoint. Given that Q1 guidance points to a 2% year-over-year decline, investors can expect sequential improvement in year-over-year growth for the remaining quarters as the benefits of key growth drivers begin to materialize.

As illustrated above, the key drivers for future growth are organic growth from its existing products, expanding its offerings as well as its target market along with a strategic approach to potential M&A opportunities. Though it is difficult to estimate the potential from M&A, I remain confident in the ability for the company to grow from cross-selling its offerings to its existing customer base. Given the already low churn of less than 1%, this should lift net retention rates well above 100%, which when coupled with new sales, should support mid-single digit overall growth rates in the medium term. Furthermore, the potential for securing larger deals as the company moves further upmarket remains significant. Beginning in Q2, management plans to share certain go-to-market metrics so that investors get a better view of the company's growth outlook. Though it was not specifically mentioned, I expect the metrics to be related to pipeline size, average deal size and win-rates.

Management has also undertaken significant investments in an ERP program aimed at improving the efficiency of the entire business, including improving productivity, quality of our service and customer support. Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $44 million for FY25 includes approximately $2 million in one-time ERP costs, which depress margins to 17%. Adjusting for this, margins would have been above 18%, which I believe represents a base above which margins should improve from FY26 onwards, given the operating leverage in the business.

With the balance sheet comprising $78 million in debt and $17 million in cash, management's primary focus remains deleveraging. In addition to lowering interest related costs, reducing debt also allows management further flexibility with regard to capital allocation for M&A and returning capital to shareholders.

SANG stock valuation

At the current share price of $6, Sangoma Technologies has a market cap of $200 million. With net debt of $61 million, its enterprise value stands at $261 million. Based on the midpoint of management's full year adjusted EBITDA guidance for FY25 of $44 million, shares are valued at an EV/adjusted multiple of 5.9. Considering my estimates for annual capital expenditures and interest payments of $4 million and $5 million respectively, I expect FCF to be around $35 million, assuming no changes in working capital. This implies a Price/FCF multiple valuation of 5.7.

I would point out that stock-based compensation expenses at close to $3 million annually, remain at reasonable levels. While revenue growth expectations are muted, earnings growth has significant potential in the coming year due to margin expansion. Given this context, I find the current valuation quite attractive, especially when compared to a peer like RingCentral (RNG), which I have previously covered. RingCentral, focusing more on the enterprise segment, is growing slightly faster at high single-digit rates but has a much higher Price/FCF multiple of 7.5, representing a 30% premium. If Sangoma can achieve consistent organic growth and gradually improve its margin profile, I believe the shares could see a significant re-rating, narrowing the gap to its peers.

Risks to consider

Competition

The cloud communication solutions market is highly competitive, with large players such as Cisco (CSCO), 8x8 (EGHT), Microsoft (MSFT) Teams, Zoom (ZM), RingCentral as well as smaller players such as Crexendo (CXDO) and IDT (IDT) owned net2phone. Therefore, competition poses a risk to Sangoma's market position and growth potential. While the larger competitors primarily target the enterprise segment, Sangoma differentiates itself by focusing on the SMB segment and leveraging its ability to bundle essential hardware together with its wide range of services.

Balance Sheet Challenges

I consider the company's net debt level to be quite manageable, with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 1.4, particularly in light of ongoing deleveraging efforts. However, given management's ambitions for M&A, there is limited capacity to take on additional leverage without compromising the strength of the balance sheet.

Dependency on partners for sales and marketing

Unlike most of its competitors that have direct sales teams, Sangoma relies on partners for its sales and marketing efforts. While this approach offers certain advantages, it also limits direct engagement with end customers, which could result in weaker brand loyalty and slower feedback loops. So far, this risk has been minimal, as indicated by the low churn rates. However, investors should pay close attention to management's commentary on partner relationships and their effectiveness in driving success.

Conclusion

I view investing in SANG stock as an appealing opportunity, particularly due to the improved outlook following the company's initial successes in transforming its go-to-market strategy. Although challenges remain in achieving consistent organic growth and stronger profitability, especially in a competitive landscape, I believe the attractive valuation offers a solid margin of safety for investors.

data.title: Funko: Turnaround Measures Are Beginning To Bear Fruit (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Though the bank earnings season has all but declared a “soft landing” for the U.S. economy, lifting the S&P 500 to fresh heights, many pockets of the economy remain strapped by weaker consumer spending. But yet, amid this backdrop, pop-culture toy manufacturer Funko (NASDAQ:FNKO) has achieved a remarkable turnaround.

Year to date, under its new CEO Cynthia Williams, a former executive of much larger toymaker Hasbro (HAS), shares of Funko have jumped more than 50% on strong results (or at least, better than feared).

I last wrote a sell opinion on Funko in December, when the stock was trading under $7 per share. I’ll acknowledge that my sell call was premature and discounted the possibility of Funko successfully turning its business around. As a reminder, late last year the company set out its intentions to trim its workforce, change its executive leadership team, and perhaps most significantly, slice down the number of SKUs in its portfolio as it decided to shrink its inventory requirements and focus on its best-performing brands. So far, this strategy appears to be yielding fruit, prompting me to raise my outlook on Funko to neutral.

The chart below gives a great summary of the changes the company is putting in place:

Early into its turnaround plan, and at current share prices near $12, I have a more balanced view of Funko’s risk-reward profile going forward. On the bright side for the company:

  • Succeeding as a smaller portfolio. The company’s decision to shrink its SKU counts has had the desired effect of reducing inventory requirements, helping the company to also trim down its debt load for working capital. In addition, the company also reached a ~$2 million settlement with a recent shareholder lawsuit (that argued Funko misrepresented the value of its unsold inventory), removing an unwanted distraction from the stock and hopefully also addressing the core issue by producing faster-moving inventory itself.
  • Achieving growth again. And despite the portfolio shrinkage as well as U.S. macro headwinds, the company is growing revenue in the single digits, thanks to outsized growth internationally: indicating Funko’s potential in expanding overseas as it strikes more deals with international resellers.

Still, we have to be mindful of a number of risks here, which include:

  • Funko is heavily exposed to fads. Toy sales pick up around certain brands when there’s a catalyst (a film release or a new season of a popular TV show, for example). Funko’s portfolio reduction also means it is more reliant on its biggest hitters to deliver strong sales around these key sales moments.
  • Debt load. Funko is carrying a meaningful amount of net debt and has minimal cash, which is risky for a company that often flips between being profitable and sustaining losses.
  • New leadership team. There’s a new CEO and CFO in the C-suite. While so far the change-up has yielded positive results, the new team is still relatively untested.

All in all, I think Funko’s YTD pop has fairly compensated the company for the beginnings of a successful turnaround, but much remains to be done. I’d advocate remaining on the sidelines here, as I don’t think there’s much incentive from either a fundamental or valuation incentive to buy a fresh position at ~$13.

Q2 recap

Let’s now go through Funko’s latest quarterly results in greater detail to demonstrate where the company has surprised investors to the upside. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Funko’s revenue grew 3% y/y to $247.7 million, decimating Wall Street’s much more conservative estimate of $231.0 million, which would have represented a -4% y/y decline.

The charts below show detail on the company’s sales by segment. Clearly, de-assortment in the core collectibles category isn’t impeding growth in the category, up 6% y/y, though its acquired subsidiary Loungefly - maker of branded accessories like backpacks - is struggling. Still, we applaud the company’s 20%+ growth in Europe and Asia, though investors need to be mindful of the contraction in the U.S., which is more than two-thirds of overall revenue.

One word of caution: the company noted that $9 million of revenue in the quarter (worth roughly four points of growth) was due to reseller customers pulling forward shipments due to logistics concerns. This means that Q2 benefited from an unnatural channel inventory build that will unwind next quarter. Without this favorable shipment timing, revenue would have been only about flat y/y. However, the company also points out that these results are admirable amid the recent SKU reduction as well as the lingering impacts of a Hollywood strike that have resulted in a dearth of new content production (Funko benefits greatly from key sales moments when new movies and films are released). Per CFO Yves LePendeven’s remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

For the second quarter, total net sales were $247.7 million. Direct-to-consumer sales mix in Q2 was 23% of our gross sales, up from 18% in last year's Q2. This represents 33% direct-to-consumer sales growth, which we achieved despite a lack of new entertainment releases due primarily to the Hollywood strikes.

Our Q2 sales included a pull forward of approximately $9 million as certain customers placed orders earlier than usual in part to secure vessel space due to rising freight costs and container availability issues. The bottom line impact was approximately $2.5 million.”

We note as well that Funko’s gross margin rose sharply to 42%, 13 points better y/y. The company benefited both from lower inventory reserves (as in, losses from unsalable inventory that impacts gross margin, a direct result of the company’s decision to terminate product lines with poor sales) as well as favorable pricing on channel sales (though we do have to keep in mind the pull forward of shipments that benefited this quarter’s revenue).

Adjusted EBITDA also hit $27.9 million, or an 11% margin: 14 points better y/y, benefiting from the company’s workforce reductions on top of the margin gains (SG&A costs were 9% lower y/y in Q2, despite the ~flat revenue growth when accounting for the pull-forward of reseller shipments).

Q3 earnings preview

Funko is set to report Q3 earnings in early November. In my view, the key focus areas for investors to watch in the upcoming earnings cycle will be:

  • U.S. contraction: does the revenue decline in the U.S. get substantially worse, and what will the company say about partners' buying behavior amid macroeconomic concerns?
  • Gross margins: can the company keep up the same pace of y/y gross margin expansion as in Q3?

Funko has stated that one of its core near-term goals is to expand its partner distribution network. In my view, we're likely to see revenue eclipse results as the company aggressively pushes shipments onto its partners, but we might see some slight softening of gross margins in light of heavier trade discounts. All in all, I view the upcoming earnings to be a neutral catalyst for Funko, with a mixed bag of both positives and negatives that investors will pick up on.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $12, Funko trades at a market cap of $648.4 million. And after we net off the $41.6 million of cash and $223.9 million of total debt on its recent balance sheet (including $90 million drawn on its line of credit for working capital, which is down roughly 25% y/y thanks to the company’s inventory shrinkage), the company’s resulting enterprise value is $830.7 million.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year, Funko is guiding to $65-$85 million in adjusted EBITDA, or a ~7% EBITDA margin. At the midpoint of the guidance range, this puts the stock’s valuation at 11.0x EV/FY24 adjusted EBITDA.

Altogether, that’s not a terrible valuation, especially as Funko still has room to deliver upside on EBITDA margins this year and next if it can continue to deliver sales growth on its reduced SG&A cost base. But still, that multiple sits against a variety of risks, including sales declines in the U.S. amid a potential macro downturn, the risk of fading fads, and a new management team that may not have the experience to turn Funko around in a crisis.

At best, I’d say Funko’s YTD rally leaves the stock fairly valued. Keep this stock on your watch list, but don’t rush to buy this name on its recent momentum.
